blocks.text: If $z$ is a function of $x$ and $y$, i.e., $z = f(x, y)$, the total differential $dz$ is obtained from the partial differentials $dx$ and $dy$ by the use of the following formula:

blocks.latex: dz = \frac{\partial z}{\partial x} dx + \frac{\partial z}{\partial y} dy
blocks.description: This formula expresses the total differential of a function of multiple variables.

blocks.text: The reason for this is more clearly seen if we derive from the fundamental idea of motion, and introduce the actually measurable quantities like $x$ and $y$.

blocks.caption: Figure 21
blocks.description: The figure illustrates the change in $z$ as it varies with $x$ and $y$, highlighting how the position on the surface transitions as the point $(x, y)$ moves around.

blocks.latex: \text{rate} \; \text{of} \; \text{change} \; in \; z = \text{change in} \; z \text{ due to the change in} \; x+\text{ change in} \; z \text{ due to the change in} \; y.
blocks.description: This relates the rates of change in the variables with respect to the total change in $z$.

blocks.text: Let $P$ be a point $(x, y)$ on a surface, and let $P'$ move to a new position $Q$ near to $P$. The change of position is most likely to be represented by a vector on the surface (e.g., keeping constant).
